speaker: interviewee
text: I want your [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can ID the tape, if you want to.

words.text: the tape, if you want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brenda Senders, tape three.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We just need to get a couple more seconds--

words.text: to get a couple more seconds--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I get a drink?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, sure. Do you want me to get you some more water? You OK?

words.text: to get you some more water?

speaker: interviewee
text: You would call it an interesting story, huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to just clear up the one point of where your uncle was at this point, and then we'll continue with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brenda, just to go back for a little bit, your uncle, who was with you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where was he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He remained down there in the woods in Karasin with his son. [? Bebik, ?] we called him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Dave. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: --now you're going to meet the larger group of partisans and fight with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And fight with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left behind everybody. I went with fight with them. They were Russians. They were Ukrainians. They were Jewish like I told you, a [? potpourri ?], a mixture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them. Maybe some did not advertising, not going out with placards on the front of them. But I tell them if they ask me. I was-- I was proud of myself, of my family. I knew it. It--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you use an assumed name, or your own name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name, Braniaslava they use to call me [RUSSIAN] You know you call, in the Russian version, you call it by your name and your father's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Continue with your story please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Braniaslava, [? Brania, ?] yes. Yes? What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue with your story, where'd you go now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left yes. In the woods, when I came back, I left my sister. And I told the woman, what I told her, she got my message straight. My sister survived. That's what I wanted to tell you, emphasize if you will forget some things. And she survived. And then I met her in my town later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Well, let's talk about your life with the partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the partisan, what the life, a day in the life, I'll give you some example. We used to occupy a village. We had our radio station with three people working. We had receiving and transmitting telegrams with Moscow. We used to have assignment what to do. And we were a might, I don't know, 1,600, 1,000, I don't know. But we were a might. The Germans were afraid of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you being supplied?

speaker: interviewee
text: The supplies, we used to ambush the Germans, go in to their bases if it's necessary, if we could. Some supplies came even by planes. That's ironic, but they used to come by planes. They used to supply us from behind the front. They used to go over the front, throw us ammunition. Sometimes they used to even-- even land the planes, if it was safe, and take some of our very wounded partisans and take them back. And they survived going through the front, not being exploded by day, by the Germans who were watching.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of training did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very fast training. I learned very fast how to use my gun. We used to go out. We had-- how to-- they-- they teach me how to-- on be to-- horseback ride. And it was natural to me, one, two, three. It was, I learned. In tough times, you learn fast. But then how to use your gun, we used to go out sometimes on-- on the-- we had a major, I remember, that used to take us-- take us out in the woods. And he used to-- to train us how to do it. But we knew it was no big deal. You put a bullet, and you kill somebody. That's all. And-- but he used it to-- to-- to exactly into the point wherever you want it, you know, to-- to do the job right. So and then to have to throw a grenade, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to use a machine gun, if it's necessary. I had a gun. In the beginning, I was given-- I remember I was given, at the beginning a long one. I had to-- I had to drag him-- had to drag him. I'm a small woman. And the-- and the gun was-- was bigger than-- than you know. Then I was-- I was graduated and I was giving one of the fights we got from the Germans. I was given a small one. A [? kaladinka ?] they call it, a small one. I was happy with it. And then to the end, I had-- after [INAUDIBLE] 71 bullet, manually, automatically, whatever the situation would call for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know how to ride a horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I learned very fast. And very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you also get training on using explosives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A grenade is nothing. To it, you just pull out and just throw a grenade.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean to derail a train for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we derailed-- we did it. In the beginning, we used to do it in one spot. But then, we-- we find out the Germans would fix them, one, two, three. So we had to derail them in two or three places. And then they used to put in their sentries, so that we would have to take him off. With our silencer, we used to take him off. You know, silencer, put a silencer on the gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far-- how far from Karasin did you travel with this band?

speaker: interviewee
text: With this band, I call them freedom fighters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freedom fighters.

speaker: interviewee
text: How far? We started from the woods of Karasin. We went in the direction of the Soviet Union that at that time was almost not far from [INAUDIBLE]. It was the Soviet side, the Russian side. Then they had an assignment from Moscow to go back and to go back through the woods into the Carpathian Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was south from where you were? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was west. No, it's not south yet, south, that's right. Southwest, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of activities did your freedom fighters and you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They go into their bases, knock the hell out of them. I say always, I said [INAUDIBLE]. I saw their army disintegrate. I saw them running in panic. I saw their blood was red as ours. And they were afraid of dying. And this was their war that we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay with the freedom fighters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll give you some example. We stopped in a village. I'll explain to you a day in a life of a partisan. Because I want you to picture yourself. They stopped in the village, and it was in the Soviet territory. And I remember we occupied a village. And-- and the village was surrounded with hills on one side and the other side was going to the woods. And I was in the group who was taking care-- in-- in-- in a-- in a-- in a fighting units, [? in ?] [? the ?] [? now ?] case, you put-- you put-- we were not the intelligent, but we call it the-- the people who would put them in a-- in a strategic point, in the entrance of the village, in the exit of the village, or both entrances, whatever you call it, and in the middle. Put 'em in strategic points, some people who was watch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anybody, we would let in people. But nobody is allowed to walk out from that village. While we are down there, nobody, you understand? And in the morning, while I was standing down there, there was this machine gun, was my officer. And I-- I-- I was down there in the group of people. There were five of them. We were standing down there right by the entrance of the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And anybody, for instance, anybody who would come into the village, we would give him a [? parole. ?] We would give him a sign. And we used to have, like, seven, for instance. We would say four. He would have to respond three. It should correspond seven. And then on top of this, we would have two names, like [? Valsekyr ?] or [? Valse ?] to be more complicated, more-- more thing. If I would say [? Valse, ?] he might respond [? Kyr. ?] If he doesn't respond, he would be killed. If he doesn't stop, killed on the spot, or had to come in into the village and stay down there. And we would find out who he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, this morning, we went in into that village. And in the morning, somebody tipped off the Germans. They came on our backs. They occupied the most strategic point. Because the hills, we were in the-- in the village and I-- and my officer came up running, and he said, we have a fight. And there was not time. There were seconds, minutes. You have to be on the [? move, ?] right then and then.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a fight. And we run out. And he said, we don't have the ammunition. The ammunition we used to carry on horse and buggy. Surplus, you understand, surplus of the-- the-- of the bullets and all those things, extra guns, if there are machine guns. And it was like five, six houses away in a-- in a-- down there in the-- in the house. I knew where it was. I said, I'm going to get it. I ran up to get the-- the thing. And they were sniping already from the hills. They were-- they were cutting us down. I went through the back, through the back alleys. I ran in down there. I got what I-- the-- the-- like the boy scouts, whatever they were, this big, I remember. They were in a big tank with-- with-- with the-- with the bullets.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bandolier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a bandolier, this one thing. But the whole-- a whole satchel, a whole-- a whole bagful. I put it, I remember, on me. And I was start running back. You know, I saw the homes already was standing down there facing the hill, already on fire. You know the homes in the Ukraine, they are from wood and from straw are the roofs. And the Germans who were standing down there, on the better positions, they start putting in fire-- fire bullets. And they're fire bullets, you understand. It started the fire one, two, three. And they start fire. I had to run on to the homes with my-- with my bullets, with my bag of bullets. I had to run in. I run in to one house, I remember, and I had to tumble through to the next house. I went through alive. And then I saw a guy, and I recognized him by his jacket. Ivanov was name. I said, Ivanov, cover me. I ran-- I yelled to him, Ivanov, cover me. Ivanov did not respond back. And I yelled at him with insults. I said, he [? coward ?]. I yelled at him with insults. Because he-- he didn't do the job. But Ivanov was dead. A sniper got straight to his head. We had the funeral the next day. I went through. I tumbled through the next house, the third house. I went through. I was pinching myself if I'm alive. I went, I remember, under a well. A well, you know, like in [? old ?] times, wooden, you know. When you put it in the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The bucket.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the bucket, you get the water. I tumbled [? underneath. ?] And then under the [? barns, ?] they're already my partners and my comrades. I ran in down there. They-- they-- they put a cross over them when they-- when they saw me. And I gave him the bullets. But this was not the end. We had to leave the town, that village. We had to leave fast. Because we knew, right within an hour, within a few hours, bombs. The Germans will come. And they will destroy the village. They will knock the hell out of everything. So we left. At-- at the quarries, we left some old horses, some old [INAUDIBLE], some old rags in the woods, around surrounding the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were told, a bunch of us, to go down by the clearing, the opening. In the woods there are clearings, you understand? And we told, a bunch of us, we should go backwards. We should go, like going in to the woods backwards. Then the plane will come to scout out. So you should see that people went in, nobody went out. And the rest of the partisan went on both sides. That's where they received them, on both sides. They went out. They left the village. And we predicted right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- they used to send after us the [NON-ENGLISH], we called it, like frame. And this was a photographical airplane. And he used to take photographs. And he saw them there, whatever he could see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you fooled them with the footprints?

speaker: interviewee
text: We can fool them, yes. They went into the woods, within kilometers, maybe a few kilometers. A kilometer is a half a mile. We were spectators to the-- to the tragedy. They made a-- nothing out of the village. And they thought that they'd killed us off. But they didn't. So we fooled them. This is one incident. There's another incident in the Carpathian Mountains. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or one time, I escaped with my life. And I had my explanation for it. That's all. And this I told them in the museum the same, maybe I'll repeat myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Tell your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were stationed again in a village. And I was in the [? Kamadanski ?] ward. For some reason, they select me. I should be in-- like to protect the general. And this was quite honorable. I don't know. They-- that's what I was assigned. [SIGHS] We were stationed in that village. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the morning, at night, we got a order that we must go with the general in the second village. We must go with him. We didn't know where. We didn't question where we were going. He-- they give us an order. We have to go with him. We went with the-- with our general to the next village. In the morning, all hell got loose. The Germans occupy. Somebody tipped them off the partisans are in there. They went on our [INAUDIBLE]. They occupied the village. High in the big hit and not being a soldier, a schooled soldier, I left my bullet belt. I said, in the morning, I'm coming. And I'm getting my bullet belt. And I'm getting back my bullet-- my bullets. You don't do it during the war. And I left it behind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While we were retreating, we used to have we call it the intelligentsia. We call it, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the people who used to come in the first one in a village to scout out it's everything. It's not Germans are waiting for us. And after, we would retreat. They would be the last one. Two of them, or three of them, whatever we could spare. See to it that everybody left. Some partisan can fall asleep, can get a stomachache, can something happen to him. That's everybody's out. The Germans could not get a hold of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of us running to the village. My commanding officer comes across to me. And I said, where is my [NON-ENGLISH]? [INAUDIBLE] my bandolier, you call it, a-- a belt-- bullet belt, he said, I left it under the log. And you better get it. If not, you're going to be court-- court martialled.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans were already in the boat-- in the-- in the village. I-- without thinking, I ran across the-- the opening, the-- the-- the thing. And then, my friend, [? Andre ?] was his name, saw me running to the village. He thought I got crazy. He ran with his pistol. He said, I'm going to kill you, you stupid. I said, my [NON-ENGLISH], my bullet belt, and he let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you imagine running up on a horse? He told me [INAUDIBLE], it-- he put it under the log by the house. I ran up. And I still get bubbles up to my-- my stomach. I ran up to that house, the log where he's told me. I had my horse as tight as I could. I ran. I find my bullet belt. I put on my head, I remember. I mounted the horse. And I was running through the woods. I was running through the clearing into the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came, I heard the screams of the people. The village was burning. The yelling of the people and the Germans was already doing-- making a mess, a mess of the people and everything. When I came into the woods, escaping from all this hell, I touched myself to see if I am right, if I am alive, or if it's something, a dream. And when I came to back to the partisans, to my-- my general said, gosh, you live a long life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was right. My only explanation for that, and this is mine, I wore-- [INAUDIBLE] they saw me, I'm positively. They saw me on a horse. My only-- my explanation I wore a German uniform from a German pilot. I remember it was, we got it once in the fights. And he was a-- must be a tall, a tall [? fritz. ?] You know, so, I had to cut off. You know, I remember cutting it off to-- to suit me. And I had a poncho, a poncho. You know with those different colors. The kids now wearing, you know. When you camouflage, you can camouflage. You fall on the ground you camouflage. With this, you don't-- you-- you don't-- when a plane comes over you, hovering over you, he doesn't see. You are a part of the ground. And I wore this pilot hat. My explanation for all this things, how that they didn't sniper me down, or they didn't kill me, because they saw me in full view. They thought that maybe I was one of them. It's the only thing I can tell you. Maybe I was lucky, I don't know. But this is my explanation for this story. This is another.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. How long did you stay with the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up till 1944, about a year and a half. But you can have to start from '42, to come to '44. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were gallant fighters. They were fighting to hell. I know [INAUDIBLE]. They were afraid of our shit. They were afraid of us. They used to-- imagine, they had the guts to call the partisans, bandits. They were the bandits. They occupy. They [INAUDIBLE]. They killed our families. They murdered. I was a Polish national. My family dates hundreds of years. I don't know. Maybe 800, 900, like we are now American nationals. That's our-- if I would go out from the country, except my division, my different, I was a-- I was a Polish national, of a Jewish faith. And all of a sudden, Hitler, this monster, made us all criminals. And he wanted to destroy a people, a culture, a people. But now I believe I don't know. Because we were nationals. We were Poles. We were [? living ?] down there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the hurt. And all the countries are sitting and playing with some toying around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now, what happened in 1944? That's when the Russians started to counterattack. And they drove the Russian-- the Germans back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them coming into our camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you when the Russian army moved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were-- we were-- we were as heroes, we were heroes. We were looked up as heroes. Because mostly the party, in my case, it was no choice. I went down there to fight because they did the horrible thing. I don't know, maybe, you understand. But in this situation, and so they gave us, you know, jobs and whatever. Even I wanted-- I was throwing the idea to go to school. I wanted to take English and-- and-- and French and whatever. And they would subsidize. They would give me some-- some-- some help for it. And they looked up. They said like after the war, for generals and for partisans, you don't have even to stand in line. You know, we had our signs, our red signs or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, this was nice. And I never heard any bad words against the United States in the partisans. And I remember when we got penicillin into the woods from the United States. Some nice words were said. And a lot of partisans were spared. And a lot of partisans were helped with that. Now, did you follow the Russian army west, back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't follow it. We did our mission, our job was accomplished after the Nazis-- after the front was advancing. Because they knew this-- the army knew that the partisans was a big help. They put 'em on their knees. They had to take off divisions from the front to fight the partisans. And I know it for a fact. They didn't have no day or no night, no rest for them. They find out fast. But not as much as I would want them to, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Sarny after the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nobody, scattered people dead in here. [POINTING] A family member, two members of a family, there and here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went a little bit to work. I was toying with the idea to go to school. But in meantime, I had to work. And then start-- we start hearing that every Polish citizen was a Polish national before the war to '39 has the right. They gave the Ukraine part of Poland, they gave to the-- to the Soviets, you understand? And the part-- the other part from the book, they gave to the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So any Polish national who lived in the Ukrainian side of Poland had the right to-- to leave and to go on the Polish side. And that's what I decided of ready. With the intention that I'll go that time to Palestine. I had my relatives. I never intended to come to the United States but to Palestine. That's-- I had some relatives. I had my aunts and some uncles-- excuse me-- that left before the war. And that was my intention was to go. It wasn't so easy to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't so easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left. We came to Poland. And there was the Bricha. If you heard about it, this is the-- it's clandestine. You read it in-- in [? Exodus. ?] And that's very much, much the same as it was. We stayed in Poland for a while. And then there was unfriendly. We were surrounded still with unfriendly people around us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying we--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know for some reason, or the jealousy, or whatever, they were-- their thing or their guilt. They didn't do nothing during the war. They didn't help their own-- their own people, their own nationals. That's what I say sometimes. You betrayed your own people. They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you link up with your sister and your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Sarny, yes. And I linked up with my sister. And then Beuthen, I wind up, I stayed with them. And I wanted my sister to go-- she was younger than I was-- to go in the orphan, [INAUDIBLE] kibbutz. So I put her in a kibbutz in Poland, near the Czechoslovakian border, l remember. Near the Czechslovak-- and I used to come from Beuthen, Ludwigsdorf or something. I don't remember. I think Ludwigsdorf they call it. And there was this kibbutz with all the orphanage [INAUDIBLE], whatever they didn't have parents. They was-- they were congregated on there. For my sister, I said, listen, if you'll have to go to Palestine, I'll come with you. Because you know I-- I was not-- she was younger than I was. And one day, I came. She was not down there. She was not there anymore. She went over the-- the border. She went on the-- on the Czechoslovakian side. And then they went on the American side. That's the way we used to do at that time. The exodus you-- you-- you-- you described down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Beuthen. Beuthen is a-- is a town in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- Yes, I think it was on the border between Poland and Germany, or it was a German town. I'm not sure. I don't-- I don't-- before the war-- I'm talking about '39. I'm talking about that time. I think it was a German town. Because Katowice-- Katowice was still a Polish town. But Beuthen, I think, it was a German town.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Beuthen, I didn't-- I left my sister. I-- my sister and I, I was very sad because I didn't find her. But then I-- I figured out what happened. We knew it already. But she went away with her group of people. Then, and-- and then later on, we left Poland. We went secretly, clandestine. We left Poland. We went over to the Czechoslovakian, over to Czechoslovakia. It was political. It was something we told them. But they looked through the-- through the fingers at that. They let us through. Because they knew what we went through, the plight and the horrible things we went through. They let us through. They were very friendly and on friendly terms, mostly of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're-- we're gonna take a temporary break. And then we'll come back and finish your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's great. We'll just take two minutes to change--
